nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons and seaward reefs in clear, coral-rich waters; depth 0–40 m

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented eating filamentous algae, small invertebrates, coral polyps, and fish eggs. In captivity, generally accepts a variety of meaty preparations including mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brineshrimp, finely chopped clam, mussel, and squid; spirulina-based foods help round out the algal portion of the diet. Feeding several small portions throughout the day tends to work better than single large meals, particularly for newly introduced specimens that can be reluctant to feed initially. This species has been reported to eat glass anemones (Aiptasia).

avoidWith: conspecifics unless introduced as a mated pair

summary: The Saddleback Butterflyfish (Chaetodon ephippium) is an Indo-Pacific species recognisable by the bold black saddle with a white border on the upper rear body. Generally considered an intermediate-level fish, it is best suited to fish-only or FOWLR systems, as it tends to nip at corals and sessile invertebrates.
careNotes:
Chaetodon ephippium is generally recognised by a prominent black saddle with white border on the rear upper body, orange on the lower face and along the tail outline, and pale grey base with fine blue-grey body stripes. Adult males have been reported to develop a filament extending from the dorsal fin. Juveniles are typically solitary and nearshore while adults occur singly, in pairs, or small groups.

This species is generally considered not reef-safe; it has been reported to nip at stony corals, some soft corals, tubeworms, and clams. It is therefore generally advised for fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) systems rather than mixed reefs. Notably it has been documented eating glass anemones (Aiptasia), which some keepers consider a beneficial trait.

Temperament is generally peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates but tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and similar-looking butterflyfish. In the wild it has been documented forming monogamous pairs; in captivity a mated pair introduced simultaneously can often be kept together.

New specimens are often reluctant feeders — offering a variety of small meaty items several times daily is advisable until the fish is established. Medium-sized specimens (~7–10 cm at acquisition) have been reported to acclimate more readily than very small or very large individuals.

Adults are typically reported in the range of 23 to 30 cm. A minimum tank of 500 L is generally recommended, as the Saddleback Butterflyfish is an active swimmer that reaches a substantial size. Lifespan data for this species is not well documented.
